questions.question: Explain the problem of vanishing gradients in basic RNNs and how LSTM addresses it.
keyPoints: Basic RNNs suffer from vanishing gradients when backpropagating through many time steps
Long chains of multiplications make gradients shrink exponentially
LSTM uses gating mechanisms (forget, input, output gates) to control information flow
Cell state acts as a highway for gradients to flow unchanged
Gates use sigmoid and tanh to learn what to remember and forget

questions.question: Differentiate between many-to-one, one-to-many, and many-to-many RNN architectures with one example each.
keyPoints: Many-to-one: multiple inputs produce single output (e.g., sentiment analysis)
One-to-many: single input produces sequence (e.g., image captioning)
Many-to-many: sequence input produces sequence output (e.g., machine translation, POS tagging)
Encoder-decoder is a special case of many-to-many with different input/output lengths

questions.question: What is the purpose of teacher forcing in RNN training? Discuss its advantages and potential drawback.
keyPoints: Teacher forcing uses ground truth previous output as input during training instead of model's prediction
Advantage: Faster convergence during training as model receives correct previous input
Drawback: During inference, model uses its own predictions which may be incorrect, causing error accumulation
Solution: Scheduled sampling gradually transitions from teacher forcing to free running

questions.question: Explain the architecture of GRU and state how it differs from LSTM.
keyPoints: GRU has two gates: Update gate and Reset gate
Update gate controls how much previous knowledge to keep vs. new information to add
Reset gate determines how much past information to ignore
GRU combines cell state and hidden state into one
GRU has fewer parameters than LSTM (fewer matrix multiplications)
GRU trains faster but LSTM may perform better on complex long-sequence tasks

questions.question: What is attention mechanism in sequence modeling? How does it solve the bottleneck problem?
keyPoints: Attention allows decoder to access all encoder hidden states at each output step
Instead of encoding entire sequence into one fixed vector, creates context from weighted sum
Weights (attention scores) determined by relevance of each input position
Solves bottleneck problem of Seq2Seq for long sequences
Enables model to focus on relevant parts when generating each output

questions.question: Derive the forward propagation equations for a basic RNN.
keyPoints: ht = tanh(Wxh·xt + Whh·ht-1 + bh) - hidden state at time t
yt = Why·ht + by - output at time t
Where Wxh is input-to-hidden weight, Whh is hidden-to-hidden (recurrent) weight
tanh squashes values to [-1,1] range
Hidden state ht captures information from all previous time steps

questions.question: Explain bidirectional RNNs with use cases where they are particularly useful.
keyPoints: Bidirectional RNN processes sequence in both forward and backward directions
Uses two separate hidden layers that combine at output
Useful when complete context requires knowing both past and future
Applications: POS tagging, NER, machine translation, speech recognition
Not suitable for real-time streaming where future input unavailable

questions.question: Explain Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) in detail. Discuss its computational complexity and methods to handle vanishing/exploding gradients.
keyPoints: BPTT unrolls RNN through all time steps into a feedforward network
Forward pass computes hidden states and outputs sequentially
Loss computed at each time step or final step
Backward pass computes gradients from last time step to first
Truncated BPTT limits backprop to k time steps for efficiency
Vanishing gradients: Use LSTM/GRU gates, proper initialization, residual connections
Exploding gradients: Gradient clipping by threshold
Complexity: O(T) time and space per forward-backward pass

questions.question: Describe the LSTM architecture in detail with equations for each gate. Explain how the cell state enables long-term memory preservation.
keyPoints: LSTM has forget gate: ft = σ(Wf·[ht-1, xt] + bf) - decides what to discard
Input gate: it = σ(Wi·[ht-1, xt] + bi), C̃t = tanh(WC·[ht-1, xt] + bc) - decides new info
Cell state update: Ct = ft·Ct-1 + it·C̃t - combines old and new information
Output gate: ot = σ(Wo·[ht-1, xt] + bo), ht = ot·tanh(Ct) - decides output
Cell state acts as information highway with only linear interactions
Linear forget gate (sigmoid near 1) allows gradients to flow unchanged
Gates learn what to store/retrieve enabling capture of long-term dependencies

questions.question: Explain the sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) architecture for machine translation. Discuss the encoder-decoder framework and how attention mechanism improves it.
keyPoints: Seq2Seq maps variable length input to variable length output
Encoder: RNN processes input sequence, final hidden state captures context
Decoder: Another RNN generates output sequence token by token
Problem: Fixed-size context vector becomes information bottleneck for long sequences
Attention mechanism: At each decoder step, compute attention weights over all encoder states
Context vector = weighted sum of encoder hidden states
Decoder uses context + previous hidden state to generate output
Attention allows model to focus on relevant input parts, handle long sequences better
Types: Global attention (all encoder states) vs Local attention (window)

questions.question: Compare LSTM and GRU architectures. Under what circumstances would you choose one over the other?
keyPoints: LSTM: 3 gates (forget, input, output) + cell state + hidden state
GRU: 2 gates (update, reset) + combines cell state into hidden state
GRU has fewer parameters (less memory, faster training)
LSTM has more expressiveness with explicit cell state
Choose GRU when: limited data, faster training needed, simpler model sufficient
Choose LSTM when: complex long-term dependencies, sufficient data, maximum performance
Empirical: Both perform similarly on many tasks, differences often negligible
LSTM preferred for tasks requiring precise memory control, GRU for resource-constrained scenarios
